methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $100M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Apptronik is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company founded by Jeff Cardenas that has raised $935M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a fragmented comp universe consisting of 1 public peer (277810.KQ) and 8 private companies in the Robotics & Manufacturing sector, including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity.

Peer-median valuation proved inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables within narrow valuation tiers—only 2 adjacent-stage peers were identified against a minimum threshold of 3. Stage-progression analysis was therefore the primary applicable method. Using the $935M+ total raised as a valuation proxy and anchoring at $100M, the analysis applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement, yielding an implied valuation of $200M.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $170M to $230M, with a median point of $200M. The range reflects 15% downside and 15% upside variance around the stage-progression point estimate. Key uncertainties include the absence of tight peer comparables in the same stage and valuation band, which limits confidence in the median figure. A subsequent round of fundraising with disclosed terms would provide critical calibration. The analysis is constrained by the broad heterogeneity within robotics subsectors and the relative scarcity of public benchmarks in this space.
